title: Church Government, Part 2: Models of Church Government and Christ as Head
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Review and Purpose
subpoints: This lesson is a subsection of a broader series on ecclesiology (doctrine of the church)
Hebrews 13:17 is the motivating text: 'Obey those who rule over you and be submissive'
You cannot obey what you don't understand; knowing church government equips you to obey God
God is a God of order (1 Corinthians 14:40): He created everything in order and governs His church in order
Review of last week: authority is good because it is an attribute of God, but can be misused

outline.title: Models of Church Government (Part 1): Episcopacy and Congregationalism
subpoints: Episcopacy: rule by bishops who govern multiple local churches; followed by Roman Catholics, Anglicans, Episcopalians, Methodists, Nazarenes, some Lutherans
The Anglican Church acknowledges that the episcopal office developed after the close of the New Testament
Independent Congregationalism: each local church is independent, governed by a democratic vote of the congregation; rarely practiced in its pure form because it is practically unworkable
Groups claiming congregationalism: Baptists, non-denominationals, Quakers, evangelical free churches
Single pastor model: the local church is independent and all decision-making authority rests with one pastor—the 'little pope' model

outline.title: Models of Church Government (Part 2): New England Congregationalism and Presbyterianism
subpoints: New England/Puritan Congregationalism: each congregation is independent from others, but the local church is ruled by a plurality of elders (not by the congregation)

Presbyterian model: local churches are connected geographically in Presbyteries; each local church is governed by a plurality of elders; churches are organized into Presbyteries, and nationally into a General Assembly
Continental Reformed churches (URCNA, RCN, Heritage Reformed, Christian Reformed) follow the same structure but use different terms: Consistory instead of Session, Classes instead of Presbytery
Confessional statements vary widely from near-nothing to the Westminster Standards or Three Forms of Unity
Presbyterianism and continental Reformed polity agree: the local church is ruled by elders in a connected system of courts

outline.title: The Bible Presbyterian Church Constitution
subpoints: The BPC Form of Government affirms that God has blessed bodies differently governed, while holding that government by presbyters (elders) is founded on and agreeable to the Word of God
Three ordinary and perpetual officers in the church: ministers, ruling elders, and deacons
Local church members are under the jurisdiction of the session (the local elders); ministers are members of the Presbytery
The BPC is organized into four Presbyteries: Great Western, Great Lakes, Eastern, and Florida
The Constitution consists of the Westminster Standards (Confession and Catechisms), the Form of Government, and the Book of Discipline

outline.title: Ordination Vows for Officers and Ministers
subpoints: Elders and deacons take vows affirming the Scriptures, the Westminster Standards, the form of government, their office, and the purity, peace, and unity of the church
Ministers take the same vows plus additional questions: confirming sincere desire for ministry, not being compelled; zeal to maintain gospel truth; faithfulness in all personal and public duties
These vows have been substantially the same since the first American Presbytery in 1706
The BPC practices 'system subscription'—requiring adoption of the Westminster system, not necessarily every detail of every proposition
Ministers may take exceptions to specific points of the Confession, which the Presbytery evaluates as core or peripheral

outline.title: Christ Is the Only Head of the Church
subpoints: Colossians 1:15-18: Christ is the head of the body, the church, so that in all things He may have the preeminence
Colossians 2:18-19 warns against not holding fast to the Head from whom all the body is nourished
Ephesians 1:22-23: God gave Christ 'to be the head over all things to the church, which is His body'
The New Testament authors themselves refused to claim headship: Paul says 'Not that we have dominion over your faith'; Peter says 'Not as lords over those entrusted to you'
Both the Roman Catholic Church (the Pope as vicar of Christ) and the Anglican Church (the monarch as supreme governor) represent departures from Scripture on this point

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro continues the Church Government series by surveying the major models of church government present in Christendom and establishing from Scripture that Christ alone is the head of His church. The lesson begins with a review of the foundational concepts of authority and submission from the previous week, anchored in Hebrews 13:17, and moves immediately into a survey of the five major models of church governance: Episcopacy (rule by bishops), Independent Congregationalism (democratic rule by the congregation), the Single Pastor model, New England/Puritan Congregationalism (elder rule in independent congregations), and Presbyterianism (connected elder rule with a system of courts).

The survey is descriptive rather than evaluative at this stage, simply mapping the ecclesiological landscape. The form of government of the Bible Presbyterian Church is then examined: its three offices (minister, ruling elder, deacon), its four Presbyteries, its adoption of the Westminster Standards with system subscription, and the specific ordination vows taken by officers and ministers—vows that have been essentially unchanged since 1706. Students are directed to bpc.org for access to all constitutional documents.

The theological climax of the lesson is the demonstration from multiple Pauline texts that Christ alone is the head of the church. Colossians 1:15-18, Colossians 2:18-19, Ephesians 1:22-23, Ephesians 4:11-16, and Ephesians 5:23 are all marshalled to establish this point. The New Testament authors themselves explicitly denied claiming headship (Paul in 2 Corinthians 1:24, Peter in 1 Peter 5:3). This stands in direct contrast to the Roman Catholic position (the Pope as vicar of Christ with full, supreme, and universal power) and the Anglican position (the monarch as supreme governor of the church). The Westminster Confession's chapter 25 is quoted approvingly: 'The claim of any man to be the vicar of Christ and the head of the church is unscriptural, without warrant in fact, and is a usurpation dishonoring to the Lord Jesus Christ.'
transcript:
## Introduction and Review

We continue our series that we began last week on the major category of ecclesiology. But we are going to be talking about a subsection of that series: church government. And you know, I don't know, some of you might wonder why waste time on this. And the reason that we're wasting time on this is because it is important. In Hebrews 13:17, the Holy Spirit says: *Obey those who rule over you and be submissive.*

So remember last week we looked at authority and we looked at submission. This particular passage brings both together: *Obey those who rule over you and be submissive, for they watch out for your souls as those who must give an account. Let them do so with joy and not with grief, for that would be unprofitable for you.* If you don't know what it means to be under authority, if you don't know what government God has appointed for His church, if you don't know what those governors are supposed to do in the church, if you don't know what your duty is toward them, you're not going to be able to obey God—and it's going to be unprofitable for you. And as shepherds of the flock, we want all things to be profitable for you.

Last week we learned that authority is good because it is an attribute of God, though it can be misused. We also learned that we all practice authority and submission—that's how God ordered things. There's no one who lives outside of the realms of authority and submission.

Today we're going to start focusing on the government of the church of Christ. And if you didn't know, the favorite Presbyterian passage in the Bible is 1 Corinthians 14:40: *Let all things be done decently and in order.* Now, this verse is in the context of the public worship of God, that God wants everything decently and in order. But it reflects the character of God. God is a God of order. He created everything in order. In recent decades, physicists have tried to put forth chaos theory as the explanation for the origin of the universe. And yet when you read chaos theory, it only works if the chaos is somewhat orderly. If the chaos is truly chaotic, then the universe wouldn't work the way it does. There. God is a God of order.

So what I would like to do today is five things. I would like to take a look at the models of church government that the church is currently using. I would like to take a brief look at the form of government of the Bible Presbyterian Church as according to our documents. I want us to realize that Christ is the head of the church. I also want us to see that the New Testament authors themselves recognized that they weren't the head of the church. And then briefly, that Christ has appointed a government for His church—not what government yet, but just that He has appointed a government.

## Models of Church Government

### Episcopacy

One model is called Episcopacy—sometimes pejoratively called Prelacy. This is a government where the church is ruled by bishops, and 'bishop' is a separate office from elder or deacon or pastor or rector. The bishop rules over several local churches that are connected to one another.

Major groups that follow this form of government include the Roman Catholic Church, the Anglicans, the Episcopalians (which is just the American version of the Anglican Church—during the Civil War, being Anglican became an issue because the head of the Church of England is the monarch), the Methodists, the Nazarenes, and some of the American Lutherans.

An interesting note: the Anglican Church acknowledges that the episcopal office developed *after* the closing of the New Testament. The reasoning goes that the authority of the bishops—being misinterpreted to have revelatory aspects—allows the church to create offices not directly found in Scripture. So it's acknowledged as a post-New Testament development.

### Independent Congregationalism

Another form of government is Independent Congregationalism, which is ruled by the congregation. Each local church is independent from every other local church. The congregation, through a democratic process, holds the ultimate decision-making power. Though this form of government is claimed by many churches, it is rarely practiced—because it just doesn't work to have the congregation make every single decision. Groups claiming this include Baptists (not all), non-denominationals, the Quakers, and Evangelical Free churches.

### Single Pastor Model

Another model is the single pastor. Each local church is independent from every other church, and the single pastor has all the decision-making authority. I was going to put it as 'the tyrant,' but then decided just to say the single pastor has all the decision-making authority. Baptists and non-denominationals often tend to follow this in practice.

### New England/Puritan Congregationalism

You might think this is the same as the above, but there is an independent congregationalist government where each local church is independent from each other, but the local church is ruled by a *plurality of elders*, not by the congregation. This is what the New England Puritans practiced. They are called Congregationalists because each congregation is independent from other congregations—but the church is ruled by elders. Even in New England, it became necessary for local churches to unite for discipline or ordination of pastors, so they formed associations that looked much like Presbyteries.

### Presbyterianism

The last model is the Presbyterian model. Local churches are connected with one another. Each local church is governed by a plurality of elders. The churches are geographically organized in Presbyteries, and all the churches in a particular nation are part of a General Synod or General Assembly. The session, the Presbytery, and the General Synod/Assembly form a system of courts where different members can appeal at different levels.

Presbyterians tend to hold to this form, but so do continental Reformed churches—the URCNA, the Reformed Church in the United States, Heritage Reformed Congregations, the Canadian Reformed, the Free Reformed, the Christian Reformed. They all hold to essentially the same form of government, just using different terms. Instead of calling the local court the 'session,' they call it the 'Consistory.' Instead of 'Presbytery,' they use 'Classis.' Some don't see these as courts in the strict sense.

These different forms of government also vary in terms of confessional statements—from almost nothing to very thorough documents like the Westminster Confession and Standards or the Three Forms of Unity.

## The Bible Presbyterian Church Constitution

So what does our Form of Government say? The BPC Form of Government says in chapter four, paragraph one and two and five:

*Almighty God in His providence has permitted that different portions of His church should be governed in various ways, and He has blessed bodies differently governed as His fruitful branches.* So we're not saying that if you're not Presbyterian, you're not blessed by God and you're a false church—that would be to deny reality.

*Rejoicing in this fact, and in no wise casting reflections upon bodies differently governed, we believe that government by Presbyters is founded upon and agreeable to the Word of God and the practice of the primitive Christians and is highly expedient.*

Paragraph two: *We believe that government by two kinds of Presbyters, ministers and ruling elders, who are to be joined in the oversight of the church, is founded upon and agreeable to the Word of God and is highly expedient.*

Paragraph five: *The ordinary and perpetual officers in the church are ministers, ruling elders, and deacons.*

So our Form of Government says there are three offices in the church: the minister, the ruling elder, and the deacon. To be 'perfectly formed'—which is just a term meaning formed as the New Testament prescribes—it must have all three offices. That's what our constitution says. And the local church is ruled by the elders under the headship of Christ.

### How the BPC Is Organized

Minsters are members of the Presbytery, not of the local church. So if a minister needs to be disciplined, it's done by the Presbytery, not the session.

All BPC churches are gathered into four Presbyteries according to our Constitution: the Great Western (anything west of Nebraska, including Hawaii and, interestingly, churches in Myanmar), the Great Lakes, the Eastern, and the Florida Presbyteries. The Great Western Presbytery regularly meets twice a year. All BPC churches in the US, Canada, and Myanmar are members of our General Synod, which ordinarily meets once a year.

### The BPC Constitution and Ordination Vows

The Constitution of the BPC consists of five documents: the theological standards (Westminster Confession, Larger Catechism, Shorter Catechism—all adopted at our founding in 1938 with amendments for a premillennial return of Christ), the Form of Government, and the Book of Discipline.

The oath that elders and deacons take:
1. Do you believe the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments to be the Word of God, the only infallible rule of faith and practice?
2. Do you sincerely receive and adopt the Confession of Faith and Catechisms of this church as containing the system of doctrine taught in the Holy Scriptures?
3. Do you approve the government and discipline of the Bible Presbyterian Church?
4. Do you accept the Office of Ruling Elder (or Deacon) in this congregation and promise faithfully to perform all the duties thereof?
5. Do you promise to study the purity and peace and unity of the church?

The minister's oath includes additional questions—including whether he has been induced by love to God and sincere desire to promote His glory, whether he will be zealous and faithful in maintaining gospel truth, and whether he engages to be faithful in all personal and public duties. These have been substantially the same since the first American Presbytery in 1706.

All these documents are available at bpc.org.

Note on subscription: the BPC practices *system subscription*—do you sincerely receive and adopt the Confession *as containing the system of doctrine*? So we allow for disagreements with specific propositions in the Confession, and the Presbytery decides whether that is a disagreement at the core of the system or a peripheral matter.

## Christ Is the Only Head of the Church

Having said all that, the most important thing to establish is this: the head of the church is not the elder, not the pastor, not the deacon, not the bishop—it is Christ Himself. Here is the one who governs. The authority church leaders have is delegated from the Lord Jesus Christ.

Colossians 1:15-18: *He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn over all creation. For by Him all things were created that are in heaven and that are on earth... He is before all things, and in Him all things consist. And He is the head of the body, the church, who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead, that in all things He may have the preeminence.*

Some have said that 'head' here means 'source'—that the church comes from Him. But it's really difficult to see that when the text also says He should have the *preeminence*, the first place, the leadership.

Colossians 2:18-19 warns against *not holding fast to the Head, from whom all the body, nourished and knit together by joints and ligaments, grows with the increase that is from God.*

Ephesians 1:17-23: Paul prays that they would know *the exceeding greatness of His power toward us who believe... which He worked in Christ when He raised Him from the dead and seated Him at His right hand in the heavenly places, far above all principality and power and might and dominion... and He put all things under His feet and gave Him to be the head over all things to the church, which is His body, the fullness of Him who fills all in all.*

Ephesians 4:11-16: God gave pastors and teachers *for the equipping of the saints for the work of ministry, for the edifying of the body of Christ, till we all come to the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the Son of God... but speaking the truth in love, may grow up in all things into Him who is the head—Christ.*

Ephesians 5:23: *For the husband is the head of the wife, as also Christ is the head of the church; and He is the Savior of the body.* The wife is to obey the husband as the church is to obey Christ—the clearest reference showing Christ is the head of the church.

### The New Testament Authors Knew This

Paul says in 2 Corinthians 1:24: *Not that we have dominion over your faith, but are fellow workers for your joy.* Peter says in 1 Peter 5:3 (speaking to elders): *Not as being lords over those entrusted to you, but being examples to the flock.* The author of Hebrews says they watch for our souls *as those who must give an account*—the account is to the Lord.

John the apostle, last living apostle, wrote: *I wrote to the church, but Diotrephes, who loves to have the preeminence among them, does not receive us... He himself does not receive the brethren... and puts out of the church those who wish to receive them. Beloved, do not imitate what is evil.* John says: this guy thinks he's the boss; he loves to be first—don't follow that example.

### Departures from Scripture

Not everybody believes what I've just taught. The Roman Catholic Church states officially: *The Roman pontiff, by reason of his office as vicar of Christ... has full, supreme, and universal power over the whole church, a power which he can always exercise unhindered.* And: *The college of bishops has no authority unless united with the Roman pontiff.*

The Anglican Church states: *His Majesty the King is the supreme governor of the Church of England. The King appoints archbishops, bishops, and deans of cathedrals on the advice of the Prime Minister.* So the monarch, not the Lord Jesus Christ, is the head of the Church of England.

Our Westminster Confession got it right, in chapter 25: *As the Lord Jesus Christ is the only head of the church, and the claim of any man to be the vicar of Christ and the head of the church is unscriptural, without warrant in fact, and is a usurpation dishonoring to the Lord Jesus Christ.*

So: the most important thing for us to take away from today's lesson is that Christ is the supreme head of the church. That's really the main thing to get. He gets to tell us what His church is to be and to do, and we look to Him for guidance and leadership. He does that through His word.

Let's pray together. Father in heaven, thank You that Your Son is our head and that He loves us as part of His church. We pray that we will be faithful to Him as we endeavor to do things according to Your word. In Jesus' name. Amen.
